title: Episode 5

jokes.text: Fleabag's rapid-fire internal commentary overlaid on her date: 'He's funny, he makes jokes. / She turned around and it was the golden one. / You sort of needed to hear the top bit.'
jokes.setup: She's narrating the date to camera as if giving a glowing review of a man she's auditioning — 'He's funny, he makes jokes' delivered as if this is a low bar being impressively cleared.
jokes.punchline: 'You sort of needed to hear the top bit' — she admits mid-stream that her positive spin on a joke he told is incomplete and possibly false, undercutting the whole sales pitch.
jokes.explanation: This is classic Fleabag form: the direct-address aside that simultaneously advances the plot and satirizes the character's own rationalizations. The self-undermining admission that we 'needed to hear the top bit' is sharp structural comedy — she's critiquing her own propaganda in real time. Scores 8+ because it perfectly embodies the show's formal conceit and could only work in Fleabag's voice.

jokes.text: Intercut commentary on the date: 'He's a feminist. — I have a sister.' / 'He's unpredictable. — I'm just gonna go for a shit.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag keeps building the man up with positive adjectives — 'feminist,' 'unpredictable' — cut immediately to his actual evidence.
jokes.punchline: 'I have a sister' as proof of feminism, and 'I'm just gonna go for a shit' as unpredictability. The gap between the label and the evidence is the joke.
jokes.explanation: This is excellent joke construction: the two-beat structure (label + deflating 'evidence') is executed twice in rapid succession with escalating absurdity. 'I have a sister' as feminist credentials is a sharp satirical observation about performative allyship; 'I'm just gonna go for a shit' as unpredictability is a perfect anti-climax. The juxtaposition format is economical and the jokes are culturally pointed. Justifies 8+ for originality and economy.

jokes.text: 'You wanna go and have sex? — That's better. / I'm really good at it. — He won't be.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag has been selling this man as charming and worthy; 'You wanna go and have sex?' is framed as him being refreshingly direct — 'That's better.'
jokes.punchline: 'I'm really good at it' from him, immediately countered by Fleabag's internal 'He won't be.' — she privately betrays her endorsement the moment he gets cocky.
jokes.explanation: The callback at 00:47 ('He's really good at it') makes this retroactively funnier — Fleabag's private cynicism is immediately disproven, which is a perfect setup for a two-beat callback. On its own, the economy of 'He won't be' as a complete character assassination is excellent. The direct-address format is essential to the joke.

jokes.text: Cut to next morning: 'He's really good at it.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag told us privately, moments ago, 'He won't be.' She was dismissive and cynical.
jokes.punchline: Cut to post-sex, single flat line: 'He's really good at it.' The callback lands because she was so wrong so quickly.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. Four words, a cut, and a complete reversal. The economy is near-perfect: it doesn't explain, doesn't linger, just delivers the correction and moves on. The callback structure (cynical prediction → immediate disproof) is elegantly constructed. Fleabag's self-aware relationship with her own unreliable narration is the show's core formal joke, and this is a textbook execution. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: 'Oh, my God...' — Fleabag to camera, post-sex, clearly rattled.
jokes.setup: She insisted he wouldn't be good. Then we got the one-line reversal. Now a seven-second scene of just her reaction.
jokes.punchline: The 'Oh, my God' is the audience's laugh confirmed: she's genuinely shaken. The brevity of the scene (it's essentially its own timestamp) means the joke is purely in the reaction beat.
jokes.explanation: This is the third beat of a three-beat sequence (won't be → he's really good → oh my God), and it earns its moment. PWB's performance carries it. The joke is structural: the show gives us a whole timestamp block just for her to say 'Oh my God,' which is a comedic commitment to the bit.

jokes.text: 'I'm not gonna be sick.' — Fleabag to camera, visually implying a hangover.
jokes.setup: Morning after. Heavy night implied. She addresses camera with clear nausea.
jokes.punchline: The declaration 'I'm not gonna be sick' delivered with the confidence of someone who is absolutely about to be sick.
jokes.explanation: Functional hangover gag. The direct-address format and physical comedy implied give it a boost, but it's a fairly standard bit. Economy is good — one line, one beat. Not exceptional.

jokes.text: 'I've got a serious appointment later. I can't miss it. It's very important. — What kind of appointment? — A serious one. Can't miss it, OK?'
jokes.setup: Stepmother announces a 'serious appointment' with obvious anxiety in the pre-wedding chaos.
jokes.punchline: When asked what kind, she simply redescribes it as 'a serious one' — circular, evasive, and reveals that she has no intention of explaining but also can't stop bringing it up.
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy — the Stepmother's controlling evasiveness is funny because she raises the topic and then refuses to engage with the natural follow-up. The circularity ('a serious one') is well-executed. Seeds mystery while being immediately comedic.

jokes.text: 'Unless you can find a way to stop this horrendous wedding from happening.'
jokes.setup: Stepmother has just snapped that she doesn't need Fleabag to do anything for her.
jokes.punchline: Immediately adds 'unless you can find a way to stop this horrendous wedding' — she's agreed to marry this man, but can't pretend she wants to. The dark honesty mid-sentence is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Excellent character moment — the Stepmother's brittle, barely-suppressed dread bleeding into a seemingly normal conversation is both funny and sad. 'Horrendous' is the key word: not 'difficult' or 'complicated' — horrendous. The self-awareness about her own choices, delivered as a throwaway, is sharp writing.

jokes.text: 'What's he ill with? — A lorry.'
jokes.setup: The Priest announces his brother is ill and he must leave to attend to him. Stepmother, controlling and pragmatic, asks what he's ill with — expecting a medical answer.
jokes.punchline: 'A lorry.' — meaning he was hit by a truck. Delivered flat, as if 'lorry' is a valid illness category. The single word does everything.
jokes.explanation: The best single joke in the episode and one of the finest in the series. The setup is mundane — a procedural question about illness — and the answer reframes the question entirely while being both darkly funny and genuinely tragic. 'A lorry' as a one-word answer to 'what is he ill with' is formally perfect: it answers the literal question (what afflicted him) while violating the semantic expectation (medical condition). The deadpan delivery makes it land harder. This is the kind of joke that earns a 9+: economical, original, character-specific (only the Priest could deliver this with that particular stillness), and it produces both laughter and a slight wince. Comparable to the highest-caliber anchor examples.

jokes.text: Fleabag's camera look: 'Oh, this is gonna be spectacular.' — knowing beat before Stepmother's reaction to the Priest's departure.
jokes.setup: The Priest has just dropped the wedding-cancellation bomb. The room goes quiet.
jokes.punchline: Fleabag looks directly at camera with gleeful anticipation before the explosion — she's narrating the family chaos to us like a co-conspirator audience member.
jokes.explanation: The fourth-wall break used at maximum structural efficiency — Fleabag as impish narrator, previewing the chaos about to unfold. 'Spectacular' is the precisely right word: it's mildly sadistic, it's accurate, and it draws us into complicity with her. Justifies 8+ because it exemplifies the show's formal conceit at its most effective and most character-specific.

jokes.text: Stepmother erupts: 'What a cunt! / Bastard! Fucking ba...! / I need to paint! I need to paint now! Send them away!'
jokes.setup: The Priest has left after the family was gracious and understanding to his face.
jokes.punchline: The door closes and Stepmother immediately detonates — escalating from 'What a cunt' to demanding to be left alone to paint as an emotional release. The specificity of 'I need to paint NOW' as a coping mechanism is the absurdist peak.
jokes.explanation: Olivia Colman (Stepmother) is at her best here. The joke is structural: the controlled politeness to the Priest's face followed by the unhinged reversal is a classic comedy of manners inversion, but 'I need to paint NOW' as an escalation destination is genuinely original. The specificity of painting as the outlet is perfectly calibrated — neither generic nor overly quirky.

jokes.text: 'Well, that solves that problem. / Well, I'm relieved for him.'
jokes.setup: The Priest has just cancelled the wedding his father is clearly being forced into. Dad and Fleabag assess the aftermath.
jokes.punchline: Dad's 'I'm relieved for him' is entirely ambiguous — relieved for the Priest who had to leave, or relieved that the wedding is off? The deliberate blurring is funny and slightly sad.
jokes.explanation: Quiet joke — Dad's passive, non-committal response to the wedding chaos is consistent with his character and contains a useful ambiguity. Not the episode's strongest but serves as a tonal breather after the Stepmother explosion.

jokes.text: The Priest is suddenly in the hall of her father's house — 'Oh, my God! / Sorry, sorry, sorry. / Jesus! How long were you there? — Literally, three seconds.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag is in the private corridor of her father's home; she turns to find the Priest unexpectedly there.
jokes.punchline: 'Literally, three seconds' — delivered to reassure her, but the fact that she was startled by three seconds is both funny and reveals the hypervigilance of their loaded dynamic.
jokes.explanation: Functional surprise-appearance gag. The comedy is mostly in the blocking (she literally does not see him until they're face to face) and in 'literally three seconds' being offered as a comfort when it's actually still alarming.

jokes.text: 'You can't just cancel a wedding. — I don't have a choice. — But you have the dress.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag pushes back on the Priest cancelling the wedding — you'd expect moral or logistical objections.
jokes.punchline: Her actual objection: 'But you have the dress.' — entirely materialist, possibly the single least spiritual counter-argument possible when talking to a priest.
jokes.explanation: Sharp misdirection — the joke works because we expect Fleabag to say something insightful about the ceremony or the relationship, and instead she goes completely practical ('you have the dress'). The absurdity of citing wardrobe as the inviolable reason a sacrament must proceed is excellent. Good economy.

jokes.text: 'Please don't come to the church again. / I mean that... with the greatest of compliments.'
jokes.setup: The Priest is telling Fleabag to stay away — which is a plea, a warning, a boundary.
jokes.punchline: 'With the greatest of compliments' — he's saying her presence is so destabilizing that he needs her absent; framing that as a compliment is both genuinely sweet and structurally absurd.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's finest lines. It works on multiple levels: it's funny (the formal contradiction), it's romantic (the implication that she's too dangerous to be near), and it crystallizes their dynamic in a single line. The pause between 'Please don't come to the church again' and 'I mean that with the greatest of compliments' is structurally perfect — the first line closes, the addition reopens it entirely. Could only come from the Priest: a man trained in formality and language, trying to express something the Church has no vocabulary for. Justifies 8+ on all counts.

jokes.text: 'It's a guinea pig. — It's a hamster. — Thanks.'
jokes.setup: He gives her a gift animal as a leaving present. She identifies it incorrectly.
jokes.punchline: He corrects her — 'It's a hamster' — and she just says 'Thanks' as if the correction changes nothing. Her non-reaction to being wrong is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Small, efficient joke. The non-reaction to correction — she just incorporates the new information with a flat 'Thanks' — is funny because it implies she'd have taken home a guinea pig equally unbothered. Maximum economy.

jokes.text: Phone call: 'I can look after the place if you like. — Really? — Well, I don't start till Monday. — Wait, why...? — I just like it.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag needs to leave the café to help her sister. A near-stranger offers to look after it.
jokes.punchline: His reason for being willing to run the café: 'I just like it.' His qualification is purely emotional with no practical grounding. Fleabag doesn't question it.
jokes.explanation: Sweet, functional joke about the casual trust that defines the café's community. 'I just like it' as a complete job application accepted without further review is lightly absurd and characterful.

jokes.text: 'Oh, sorry, it's my sister. She's a bit mental.'
jokes.setup: Claire calls in obvious distress.
jokes.punchline: 'She's a bit mental' — a casually dismissive summary that, while affectionate, will be retroactively revealed as understatement (the hair catastrophe). Classic British sibling minimization.
jokes.explanation: Functional setup for the Claire scene; 'a bit mental' is a fairly stock British comedic register. Works in context, not remarkable in isolation.

jokes.text: Claire's haircut reveal: 'I look like a pencil. — You... you don't look like... — Don't laugh!'
jokes.setup: Claire has gone to a hairdresser and clearly received something catastrophic. We see the result before she describes it.
jokes.punchline: 'I look like a pencil' — the self-diagnosis is perfect because it's both accurate and hopelessly specific. Fleabag visibly trying to suppress a laugh before being caught is the secondary beat.
jokes.explanation: 'I look like a pencil' is a brilliant piece of self-description — specific enough to be visually accurate (sharp, thin, uniform, utterly without warmth), funny as an image, and delivered by Claire whose precise corporate persona makes the comparison even more apt. The 'don't laugh' beat into Fleabag actually laughing is classic sister comedy. Justifies 8+ for character integration and quotability.

jokes.text: 'Have you been drinking? — He gave me champagne before he ruined my life. — That's how they get you.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag notices Claire seems slightly unsteady; turns out the hairdresser plied her with champagne before the catastrophic cut.
jokes.punchline: 'That's how they get you.' — Fleabag's conspiracy-theory-level deadpan condemnation of hairdresser champagne tactics.
jokes.explanation: 'That's how they get you' applied to hairdresser champagne is a perfect small joke: it escalates 'hairdresser gives you bubbly' into a predatory long-con, and the deadpan delivery makes it feel like genuine wisdom. Economical, quotable, slightly absurdist in its framing of hairdressers as arch-manipulators.

jokes.text: 'Did you go to Anthony? — Claire. — I know.'
jokes.setup: We've apparently been warned about Anthony the hairdresser before. Claire's 'I know' implies prior warnings were given and ignored.
jokes.punchline: The economy of the exchange: Fleabag needs only one word ('Claire'), Claire needs only two ('I know') to confirm the entire history of a bad recurring decision. It's implied we've been here before.
jokes.explanation: Three words do the work of a five-minute backstory. The audience fills in everything: Anthony has form, Claire returns anyway, Fleabag has warned her, Claire is too proud to admit it until now. Pure character comedy delivered through the most economical possible means. This is the best siblings-have-history shorthand in the episode.

jokes.text: Confronting Anthony: 'No! That is exactly what she asked for. — No, it's not. We want compensation. — Hair isn't everything. — Wow. / What? / Hair is everything.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag storms Anthony's salon to get compensation. Anthony insists the cut was requested. Fleabag prepares a speech.
jokes.punchline: Anthony's dismissal 'hair isn't everything' triggers Fleabag's aria about hair. The journey from minor dispute to full cultural manifesto is the escalation.
jokes.explanation: The setup — Fleabag and Claire storming a salon — is good comedic staging. The payoff builds to the 'hair is everything' speech, which transitions the scene from comedy into something more emotionally real. Funny in its escalation, but the speech itself moves slightly away from pure comedy into drama.

jokes.text: 'Hair is everything. We wish it wasn't so we could actually think about something else occasionally but it is. It's the difference between a good day and a bad day...'
jokes.setup: Anthony has dismissed the importance of hair. Fleabag disagrees.
jokes.punchline: The speech escalates from personal observation to feminist cultural analysis ('We're meant to think that it's a symbol of power, symbol of fertility. Some people are exploited for it and it pays your fucking bills') — arriving at 'Hair is everything, Anthony' as a devastating concluding address.
jokes.explanation: This is writing that crosses the comedy/drama line — it's funny as a pompous overreaction, but true in its feminist cultural commentary, and the 'Anthony' address at the end gives it formal closure. The humor comes from the gap between the trigger (a bad haircut in a salon) and the response (dismantling beauty industry politics), but the speech is also genuinely correct, which makes it resonate beyond a punchline. Justifies 8+ for originality and character integration.

jokes.text: 'Claudia, bring me the bin.' — Anthony's response to the speech.
jokes.setup: Fleabag has delivered a passionate feminist manifesto about hair. The room waits.
jokes.punchline: Anthony silently processes, then calls for the bin — presumably to show Claire's reference photo from the bin, but also functioning as a total dismissal of everything Fleabag just said.
jokes.explanation: Four words after a minute of impassioned speech, and they do everything. 'Claudia, bring me the bin' is a perfect deadpan understatement response: Anthony isn't moved by the speech, he's simply proceeding to prove his point. The economy is extraordinary — the joke is that he bypasses the entire emotional freight of what was said and goes directly to logistics. The bin as the next plot beat (presumably containing the reference photo) pays off the comedy AND the drama simultaneously. This is professional-grade joke construction.

jokes.text: 'If you want to change your life, change your life. It's not gonna happen in here.'
jokes.setup: Claire apparently did ask for the cut. Anthony is now philosophizing about why people come to hairdressers expecting transformation.
jokes.punchline: The line is both genuinely wise and a devastating dismissal — he's just told them they're in the wrong place for what they're actually seeking. Delivered as mild hairdresser aphorism.
jokes.explanation: Anthony, a hairdresser, delivering unsolicited life philosophy as the emotional resolution to a haircut dispute is wonderfully absurd. 'It's not gonna happen in here' is the kicker — the salon as a place where real transformation is explicitly not on offer. Good economy, quotable, slightly unexpected coming from the character position of 'angry hairdresser.'

jokes.text: 'You got any cigarettes? — No. — Good.'
jokes.setup: Post-salon, the sisters decompress. Fleabag asks for cigarettes.
jokes.punchline: Claire says no, then 'Good' — she's both being sisterly (you shouldn't smoke) and also reveals she wanted one herself. Or she simply congratulates herself. The reading is deliberately ambiguous.
jokes.explanation: A tiny, compressed sibling joke. 'Good' as Claire's addendum is the beat that makes it — she's policing the request and approving her own denial in one word. Economy near-perfect.

jokes.text: 'I didn't want my husband's baby. Isn't that awful?'
jokes.setup: Claire is processing the miscarriage; the conversation has turned serious.
jokes.punchline: The admission — genuinely dark — is delivered with the cadence of someone reporting a minor social faux pas ('Isn't that awful?'). The register of 'isn't that awful' applied to a profound revelation is the comedic element.
jokes.explanation: This is Fleabag at its most characteristic: the devastatingly dark admission framed in the most understated possible language. 'Isn't that awful?' applied to not wanting your husband's child has the same formal structure as asking 'isn't it dreadful' about a minor inconvenience. It's both genuinely funny and genuinely devastating, which is the show's entire aesthetic. Justifies 8+ for character integration and tonal precision.

jokes.text: 'What does he do? — He's a priest.' — beat — Claire's silent reaction.
jokes.setup: Fleabag says she's met someone. Claire is excited — 'What does he do?' — expecting a normal answer.
jokes.punchline: 'He's a priest.' The beat that follows — Claire processing this — is the secondary joke. The word 'priest' doing all the work.
jokes.explanation: The joke is entirely structural — the setup is the most normal question possible ('What does he do?') and the answer violates every expectation. The silence after is essential: Claire's processing is the punchline extension. The extended beat (the transcript leaves a gap before 'Is it...? Yeah.') suggests a sustained reaction hold that amplifies the comedy.

jokes.text: 'You're a genius. You're my fucking hero.' — Claire, to Fleabag, about falling for a priest.
jokes.setup: Fleabag expects sympathy or concern; Claire responds with admiration.
jokes.punchline: Claire's framing of the priest situation as a masterwork — 'genius,' 'hero' — is both ironic (it's a disaster) and revealing: she genuinely envies the chaos and passion of it relative to her own trapped marriage.
jokes.explanation: The joke layers: it's funny as an ironic reaction, and touching as a window into Claire's own imprisoned emotional life. 'My fucking hero' for the person who fell in love with a priest is the kind of bittersweet absurdism the show excels at. Claire's admiration for Fleabag's catastrophic life choices is a recurring dynamic, but this specific expression of it lands particularly well.

jokes.text: Klare's arrival and her reaction to Claire's hair: 'Claire, I love your hair! It's so cute and edgy and cool. Like superstar. Pop star.'
jokes.setup: Claire has been in agony about the haircut all episode.
jokes.punchline: A beautiful Scandinavian woman — crucially named Klare — immediately loves it without prompting, using every word Fleabag tried unsuccessfully ('edgy, cool'). The joke is that the rebranding worked, but not through Fleabag's advocacy.
jokes.explanation: The callback is well-constructed: the exact words Fleabag used that Claire rejected ('edgy, cool') land perfectly from a glamorous stranger. The joke about what legitimizes a compliment (who says it rather than what they say) is quietly sharp. Claire's immediate transformation from miserable to pleased is a lovely character beat.

jokes.text: 'These penguins are taking me to this new, amazing London music thing.'
jokes.setup: Klare (the Finnish woman) runs into them on the street.
jokes.punchline: She refers to her companions as 'penguins' with complete casual confidence — either a Finnish linguistic quirk, a nickname system, or just her worldview. No explanation is offered.
jokes.explanation: The unexplained 'penguins' is a great character detail for Klare — it's either ESL oddity or unique perception, and the lack of clarification is the right choice. Any explanation would kill it. Efficiently weird.

jokes.text: 'Oh, I have to take my step-son his bassoon. / I mean, I wish I could.'
jokes.setup: Klare invites Claire out. Claire declines citing the bassoon obligation.
jokes.punchline: 'I mean, I wish I could' — she clearly doesn't wish she could. She's relieved to have the bassoon as cover for avoiding both Klare and her own life.
jokes.explanation: The bassoon as escape hatch is a nice recurring comic device in this episode. 'I mean, I wish I could' applied to someone obviously grateful to escape is a good small irony beat.

jokes.text: Fleabag volunteers to take the bassoon: 'No biggie.' — Claire gives it up instantly.
jokes.setup: Claire was using the bassoon as her excuse to avoid a family obligation. Fleabag takes it away.
jokes.punchline: Claire's instant surrender of the bassoon — she was never going to deliver it, it was purely a social alibi — and Fleabag's 'Please get in the way of her family days' to camera.
jokes.explanation: The meta-commentary 'Please get in the way of her family days' is a direct-address joke that works on multiple levels — Fleabag addressing us about Claire, and also quietly acknowledging that Claire's family life is the thing to escape. The bassoon-as-alibi reveal is a clean structural beat.

jokes.text: Klare's compliments on Claire's hair turn her around completely: 'Goes so well with your top. — Oh, thank God. That's so sweet of you. / Honestly, I've had such a day with it.'
jokes.setup: Claire has spent the whole episode devastated by her hair. Klare (who has no stake in English social dynamics) compliments it sincerely.
jokes.punchline: Three genuine compliments from Klare and Claire is completely won over — 'Oh thank God' — revealing that her devastation was really about fear of social judgment, and any authentic validation dissolves it instantly.
jokes.explanation: A satisfying callback to the haircut crisis: the resolution is not a refund or revenge, but simply genuine appreciation. The comedy is in Claire's instant conversion — she was furious for hours, then 'goes so well with your top' ends it. It's both funny and a real insight into how people work.

jokes.text: Leaving Customer has named the hamster 'Stephanie': 'Could you give Hilary and, um... / No, don't bring Hilary. — Stephanie. / ...Stephanie, some cucumber at 2:30? / Who's Stephanie?'
jokes.setup: He's been left in charge of the café and has already developed a full relationship with the animals, including giving the new hamster a name and a cucumber schedule.
jokes.punchline: Fleabag doesn't even know who Stephanie is — he's named the hamster she just got without telling her. The integration of Hilary (the existing guinea pig) into the cucumber routine is an escalation.
jokes.explanation: The escalation of the café-sitter from casual volunteer to someone who has named the hamster and established a vegetable schedule within an hour is genuinely funny. 'Who's Stephanie?' is the perfect straight-man response. Good running gag development.

jokes.text: 'My wife would love Chatty Wednesdays. Love them.' — the leaving customer.
jokes.setup: He's wrapping up his café stint, mentioning his wife.
jokes.punchline: He emphasizes 'Love them' with a slight excess of certainty about his wife's hypothetical enjoyment of a café event she has never attended — the overclaim is the joke.
jokes.explanation: A modest callback to the Chatty Wednesday running gag. The overclaim about his wife's enthusiasm is a small character moment. Functional but not especially crafted.

jokes.text: 'He took the pinny.' — Fleabag, alone, watching him leave.
jokes.setup: The café-sitter has left. Fleabag notices he's taken the apron.
jokes.punchline: 'He took the pinny' — delivered with the exact tone of someone noting an endearing overstep. He didn't just run the café, he emotionally adopted it, which the pinny confirms.
jokes.explanation: Three words, but they do the work of a character arc: this man came to say goodbye and left having named a hamster, established a cucumber schedule, and now taken ownership of the apron. 'He took the pinny' as the single observed detail is a perfect miniature joke. Maximum economy.

jokes.text: 'He's gonna make a bassoon joke. — Is that a bassoon in your hand or are you just pleased to see me?'
jokes.setup: Fleabag sees her Dad and pre-empts his joke to camera: 'He's gonna make a bassoon joke.'
jokes.punchline: He makes exactly the bassoon joke she predicted, word for word almost. Her direct-address prediction and its immediate fulfillment is the comedy — as much as the joke itself.
jokes.explanation: The meta-joke (warning us before the joke lands) is the best use of the direct-address format here: Fleabag knows her father so completely that she can preview his comedy. The joke Dad makes is a classic dirty wordplay joke, but it's funnier because we've been prepared. Her 'Would you say that to your son?' is the necessary straight-line that allows the escalation.

jokes.text: 'Would you say that to your son?' / 'When he has his bassoon, sure.' / 'But... / He's never pleased to see me, so... / It doesn't even make sense.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag challenges the bassoon joke.
jokes.punchline: Dad's aside — 'He's never pleased to see me, so...' — pivots from the joke into genuine pathos before Fleabag cuts it off with 'It doesn't even make sense,' protecting both of them from the moment.
jokes.explanation: This is a superb dramedy beat. The joke begins as a playful deconstruction of the pun ('it doesn't even make sense') but briefly cracks open to reveal Dad's real pain — his son doesn't want to see him. Then Fleabag shuts the moment down, not cruelly but protectively. The comedy and the pain arrive in the same sentence. 'He's never pleased to see me, so...' is one of the episode's most loaded lines, and delivering it in the context of a bassoon pun is exactly what Fleabag does best. Earns its 8+ score.

jokes.text: Bassoon joke extended: '...if I was walking towards you with an amputated dick in my hand you'd think I was horny? — Well, I'd assume that you had been. / Certainly wouldn't put it past you to chop a dick off.'
jokes.setup: The dad's 'bassoon/pleased to see me' joke is being deconstructed by Fleabag.
jokes.punchline: The deconstruction escalates to 'amputated dick' and Dad's response 'I'd assume that you had been' — he doesn't defend the logic, he simply evaluates her propensity for castration. 'Wouldn't put it past you to chop a dick off' is the peak.
jokes.explanation: The deconstruction of the bassoon joke into a castration scenario is an unexpected escalation from a mild pun. Dad's 'wouldn't put it past you to chop a dick off' is a genuinely funny character-specific line — it reveals his low-key assessment of his daughter while being tonally absurd. Good escalation comedy.

jokes.text: 'I would never hurt the guinea pig.' — Martin, alone with the hamster, clearly addressing the camera.
jokes.setup: Fleabag has told Martin not to hurt the guinea pig (it's a hamster). He's been confrontational and threatening.
jokes.punchline: His dignified, sincere protest that he would never hurt the guinea pig — in a scene where he's just made multiple actual threats against Fleabag — is an absurd misplacement of moral priorities.
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the moral hierarchy: Martin has just been menacing and self-pitying in equal measure, and the thing he draws the line at — 'I would never hurt the guinea pig' — is the animal. It's both character-consistent (men like Martin maintain strange points of honor) and structurally funny (the lesser concern treated as the greater).

jokes.text: 'Oh, that's fucking adorable.' — Fleabag's reaction to Martin with the hamster.
jokes.setup: Fleabag observes Martin being gentle with the hamster despite having just been aggressive with her.
jokes.punchline: 'Oh, that's fucking adorable' — direct address to camera, deploying profanity as tenderness. The contrast between Martin's threatening behavior and his animal gentleness is the visual joke.
jokes.explanation: Good visual beat. The direct-address camera aside is economical. Fleabag acknowledging the absurdity of Martin's contradiction ('awful to humans, sweet to hamsters') is a functional, characterful joke.

jokes.text: 'Cute tables. — Oh, Jesus, Martin. — Fuck off!'
jokes.setup: Martin is in Fleabag's café, having followed her. He pivots to complimenting the decor.
jokes.punchline: His 'cute tables' as a social reset after a confrontational scene is transparently inadequate — Fleabag's 'Oh Jesus, Martin' confirms she sees through it. His 'Fuck off!' in return is the final word.
jokes.explanation: Martin saying 'cute tables' mid-confrontation is a perfect character comedy beat — he doesn't know how to de-escalate except through compliments that are hilariously insufficient. 'Oh, Jesus, Martin' is a perfectly calibrated response.

jokes.text: 'You are the problem, you know that? You are the problem in my perfect, awful life.'
jokes.setup: Martin is confronting Fleabag about her influence on Claire.
jokes.punchline: 'My perfect, awful life' — the oxymoron is both self-aware and self-pitying simultaneously. He knows it's awful; he's committed the contradiction to a single phrase.
jokes.explanation: 'My perfect, awful life' is a precise, quotable articulation of how people who create their own misery understand their situation. It's also immediately funny because he's saying this to Fleabag while threatening her in her own café. The compression of the oxymoron into three words is very good writing.

jokes.text: 'I can't even get the woman pregnant, and then you come in showing off about your miscarriage.'
jokes.setup: Martin is listing his grievances against Fleabag.
jokes.punchline: 'Showing off about your miscarriage' — the accusation that Fleabag is boasting about losing a pregnancy is so tonally wrong as to be darkly hilarious. It reframes grief as competitive display.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's finest jokes and justifies a near-9 on craft. 'Showing off about your miscarriage' is a perfect encapsulation of Martin as a character: his wounded male ego has found a way to make Fleabag's miscarriage a form of one-upmanship against him. The phrase 'showing off' applied to a miscarriage is so deeply wrong — and so completely authentic to how Martin's mind works — that it produces a laugh that's also slightly nauseating. Exceptionally original, perfectly character-specific, economical. Could only come from Martin. Comparable to the top-tier anchor examples.

jokes.text: 'Is she leaving me? — I hope so. — Don't let her leave me.'
jokes.setup: Martin has been confrontational; now suddenly asking Fleabag — his enemy in his narrative — if his wife is leaving.
jokes.punchline: Fleabag says 'I hope so,' then Martin immediately pivots to 'Don't let her leave me, please' — the reversal from threat to plea in 15 seconds is both pathetic and darkly funny.
jokes.explanation: The tonal collapse from aggressor to supplicant — asking the person you've just threatened to intercede on your behalf with your wife — is structurally excellent. 'I hope so' followed by 'Don't let her leave me please' in immediate succession is a perfect comedy beat that's also completely real. Justifies 8+: character-specific, structurally sharp, earns its emotional impact.

jokes.text: 'I hope she doesn't come home tonight.'
jokes.setup: Martin has just begged Fleabag not to let Claire leave him.
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's direct-address to camera: 'I hope she doesn't come home tonight' — a flat, sincere reversal of his wishes, delivered to us alone.
jokes.explanation: Fleabag's aside directly contradicting Martin's request is a perfect deployment of the direct-address format: she can say to us what she can't say to Martin's face. The economy ('I hope she doesn't come home tonight' as a full position statement) is excellent. The joke lands harder because it follows immediately on Martin's vulnerability.

jokes.text: 'Ah! Cashmere, cashmere, cashmere...' — Fleabag interrupted mid-thought by something on the cashmere.
jokes.setup: The confrontation with Martin has just ended; there's an emotional beat.
jokes.punchline: The cashmere becomes the sudden priority — something has gotten on her coat, interrupting what seemed like a reflective moment. The mundane intrusion on drama is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The sudden pivot to material concern (cashmere!) after emotional intensity is a classic Fleabag tonal reset. 'Cashmere, cashmere, cashmere' as a triple iteration suggests mild panic about a stain. Functional tonal break.

jokes.text: 'I will take you down, fucker.' — Fleabag to Martin, then the mutual escalating 'Fuck you!'s.
jokes.setup: The scene was heading toward a tense farewell.
jokes.punchline: Instead it escalates to mutual 'Fuck you!'s — two adults shouting at each other in a café like children. The mutual escalation with neither party backing down is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The mutual profanity escalation is funny in its regression (two adults, café, 'fuck you / fuck you') but not especially original. The release valve function of the scene works dramatically.

jokes.text: 'I will take you down, fucker.' — Fleabag, immediately after the cashmere moment.
jokes.setup: Martin has been threatening and pathetic alternately; Fleabag matches and raises his energy.
jokes.punchline: 'I will take you down, fucker' — delivered presumably with genuine physical intent, but also completely over-the-top for a café confrontation. The commitment is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Works as escalation and character beat. Fleabag's directness — no hedging, no irony, just a direct threat — is funny because she means it. 'Fucker' as the noun is perfect: specific, expressive, exactly her register.

jokes.text: 'You better start sleeping with a lawyer! — I'm already sleeping with a lawyer! — Yeah? Lucky lawyer!'
jokes.setup: Martin threatens Fleabag with legal action — 'you better start sleeping with a lawyer.'
jokes.punchline: Fleabag reveals she already is sleeping with a lawyer. Martin's 'Lucky lawyer!' is the final beat — he can't help pivoting to an involuntary compliment.
jokes.explanation: This is a very well-constructed joke. Martin's threat accidentally requires Fleabag to reveal information that deflates the threat entirely. 'Lucky lawyer!' from Martin — his involuntary shift from adversary to inadvertent admirer — is both funny and characterful. The callback to the opening scene (the lawyer she slept with last night) rewards attentive viewers. Martin's incapacity to maintain a single consistent position is perfectly illustrated. Justifies 8+ for structure and economy.

jokes.text: 'Shit.' — Fleabag, alone after Martin leaves.
jokes.setup: The confrontation with Martin is over. She assesses.
jokes.punchline: One word. Her full assessment of the scene.
jokes.explanation: A one-word scene-ender that functions as both comedic beat and genuine assessment. The joke is in the understatement: 'shit' as the complete summary of everything that just happened. Maximum economy, functional punchline.

jokes.text: 'Shit.' — Fleabag's immediate reaction after the confrontation, implying she's broken/spilled something or realizes she's made a mess.
jokes.setup: The confrontation with Martin has just concluded at high intensity.
jokes.punchline: The single word 'Shit' — the morning-after of the adrenaline, possibly surveying physical damage — is a perfect deadpan capper.
jokes.explanation: The single-word reaction as tonal reset. Works through timing and placement rather than content. Functional.

jokes.text: Fleabag's monologue: 'You know that feeling when the Hot Misogynist who might not be a misogynist is turning up at your house for the second time in 48 hours to give you nine orgasms you don't want...'
jokes.setup: Fleabag is setting the scene for the audience: she's expecting the lawyer.
jokes.punchline: '...nine orgasms you don't want' — the comedy is in the specificity (nine, not eight or ten) and the counterintuitive framing (orgasms as an imposition rather than pleasure), which reveals her true emotional preoccupation.
jokes.explanation: This monologue is the emotional engine of the episode and contains some of its best comedy. 'Nine orgasms you don't want' is a masterfully counterintuitive formulation — the specificity of nine, the idea of unwanted sexual pleasure as a burden, and the implicit confession that the Priest is the only person she wants to see — all delivered in a single run-on sentence while presumably covered in coconut oil on her doorstep. Justifies 8+ across the board: highly original, perfectly character-integrated, exceptionally economical for the density of information conveyed.

jokes.text: '...cover yourself in coconut oil and hope he hasn't noticed that you haven't shaved your...' — then the doorbell.
jokes.setup: The monologue is building toward a confession about self-grooming prep.
jokes.punchline: The doorbell cuts her off mid-sentence, so we never hear what she hasn't shaved. The interruption is the punchline — the show denies us the completion and provides it visually.
jokes.explanation: The interrupted sentence is a classic formal joke that requires the audience to complete the image themselves. PWB uses timing here as a craft element: the cut before the noun forces the audience's imagination to fill the gap, which is funnier than any word she could have said. Structurally elegant, character-consistent. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Fleabag's internal monologue: 'You know that feeling when the Hot Misogynist who might not be a misogynist is turning up at your house for the second time in 48 hours to give you nine orgasms you don't want...'
jokes.setup: Fleabag is setting up the scene of the Hot Misogynist arriving.
jokes.punchline: The monologue begins as if setting up a relatable 'you know that feeling' — the most mundane sitcom construction — then fills it with specific, extreme content ('nine orgasms you don't want', 'get your head out of the fact that the only person you want to see is the priest'). The specificity of 'nine' is a callback.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's finest passages. The 'you know that feeling' construction is a classic direct-address comedy device, but filling it with completely un-relatable specifics (nine orgasms, covering yourself in coconut oil, hasn't shaved) makes it explode. The parenthetical admission — 'the only person you want to see in the world is the priest that you...' — is doing real emotional work while being funny. The trailing 'Hey.' as the monologue is interrupted by the actual arrival is perfect. Structure is a 9 because the form (universal → hyper-specific → interrupted) is executed flawlessly.

jokes.text: 'Water? — No, thanks. I wanna keep a clear head.'
jokes.setup: The Priest is at Fleabag's home, clearly in an emotional state about something.
jokes.punchline: 'I want to keep a clear head' about a glass of water is a slight overclaim — it implies he's making life decisions tonight that require full cognitive function, which the offer of tap water does not threaten. It's also gently ominous.
jokes.explanation: A modest but well-calibrated joke: the intensity of 'I want to keep a clear head' as a response to 'water?' is funny in its overclaim and also reveals the Priest is bracing for something significant. Good character-specific comedy.

jokes.text: 'Uh, I've changed my mind about the wedding. I can't let them down like that. And apparently no one else will wear the outfit.'
jokes.setup: The Priest is explaining his decision to return to officiate the wedding despite his brother's accident.
jokes.punchline: 'Apparently no one else will wear the outfit' — after the spiritual reasoning, the purely sartorial final reason is the deflating kicker. No one else will put on the outfit.
jokes.explanation: A lovely callback to Fleabag's earlier 'but you have the dress' — that throwaway materialist objection has apparently worked its way into the Priest's actual reasoning. Or perhaps 'the outfit' is genuinely the tiebreaker for his decision. Either reading is funny. 'No one else will wear the outfit' as the kicker after moral reasoning is a perfectly calibrated anti-climax. Justifies 8+ for structure and character integration.

jokes.text: 'You gonna take off your coat? — Oh, no, no, I'm good. I'm a bit chilly.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag said earlier she's 'only got underwear under this coat.'
jokes.punchline: 'I'm a bit chilly' as her excuse not to remove the coat — technically true, in that she's barely dressed. The visual joke is that she's in coat-over-underwear while having a serious conversation with a priest.
jokes.explanation: A sustained visual gag — the audience knows why she won't remove the coat, the Priest doesn't. 'I'm a bit chilly' as a technically-true alibi while she's clearly underdressed for an important conversation is a well-managed running joke.

jokes.text: 'Do you want to get that? — Oh, no. I don't like opening the door to people I don't know. — I'm back!'
jokes.setup: Someone knocks at the door; Fleabag declines to answer with a principled-sounding reason.
jokes.punchline: The voice calling 'I'm back!' from the other side is the Hot Misogynist — clearly someone she knows. Her stated reason was a lie, and now he's announced himself, cornering her. The specific timing of 'I'm back!' after 'I don't like opening the door to people I don't know' is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The instantaneous disproof of her excuse (she doesn't open doors to people she doesn't know; he announces himself as someone she definitely knows) is a clean, well-timed bit. The joke is in the timing of 'I'm back!' arriving at exactly the right moment to destroy her cover. Good structure.

jokes.text: 'my priest is here, and he really needs some guidance.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag is trying to get rid of the Hot Misogynist at the door while the Priest is inside.
jokes.punchline: 'My priest' — the possessive is the joke. She's claimed ownership of a priest, which is both wrong (he's the priest) and reveals how she thinks of him. Also 'he really needs some guidance' — she's framing the Priest as requiring her pastoral care, which is an inversion.
jokes.explanation: 'My priest' and 'he really needs some guidance' as a double-inversion joke (she's not guiding him, he's the guide; she doesn't own him, but she wants to) is sharp and character-specific. The casual possessive applied to a priest is immediately funny.

jokes.text: 'No, I'm hoping he's having an emotional crisis.'
jokes.setup: Hot Misogynist asks if the Priest is OK.
jokes.punchline: 'I'm hoping he's having an emotional crisis' — Fleabag's honest admission that she's rooting for his existential breakdown, delivered with complete sincerity.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional line. 'I'm hoping he's having an emotional crisis' is both wildly inappropriate (she's hoping for his suffering) and completely honest (she wants him to cross the line to her). The dark sincerity — delivered as if it's a perfectly reasonable thing to hope — is vintage Fleabag. Highly quotable, character-specific, and captures the show's tonal signature. Justifies 8+ on all fronts.

jokes.text: Hot Misogynist's repeat confidence: 'I'm really good at it. — Yes, I know you are. — I'm really good at it. — Yeah, I know you are. — I'm really... — OK, you're really good at it. — Well, clearly I'm not, if you don't want it.'
jokes.setup: He keeps repeating his sexual prowess claim in response to rejection. Fleabag keeps confirming it.
jokes.punchline: His eventual pivot to 'Well, clearly I'm not, if you don't want it' — the confidence collapses the moment the evidence (her disinterest) contradicts his self-image. Then Fleabag has to rebuild him.
jokes.explanation: This is the third beat of the 'really good at it' running gag that's been going since the cold open. The escalating repetition (he won't be → he is → he keeps saying he is → he doubts it), with Fleabag trapped having to validate him mid-Priest-crisis, is an excellent sustained bit. The collapse from confidence to insecurity and Fleabag's exasperated 'You're the best sex I've ever had' coming out of sheer necessity is very well-built.

jokes.text: 'You're the best sex I've ever had. — What? — You're the best sex I've ever had. — Really? — Honestly, you... you made me come nine times.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag needs to dismiss the lawyer quickly. He's fishing for validation after his confidence collapsed.
jokes.punchline: She escalates immediately to 'you're the best sex I've ever had' — and when he demands clarification, doubles down with 'you made me come nine times.' The nine-times callback is both true (we know from earlier) and deployed here as an instrument of dismissal.
jokes.explanation: The nine-times number has now been established, confirmed, and deployed three times in the episode with increasing effect. Using 'you made me come nine times' as both a factual statement AND a tool to get rid of someone is structurally satisfying. The comedy of her sincerely saying the nicest possible sexual thing as a get-out-of-my-flat manoeuvre is excellent character writing.

jokes.text: 'Nine times. / You're a saint.'
jokes.setup: The lawyer has left. Fleabag addresses us — presumably about the Priest, reflecting on him waiting inside while she managed this.
jokes.punchline: 'Nine times. / You're a saint.' — whether directed at the Priest (who waited while she handled this) or as a private assessment, the juxtaposition of 'nine times' with 'you're a saint' applies 'saint' to the sexually patient party.
jokes.explanation: The word 'saint' applied here is both genuinely ironic (directed at the actual Priest in her living room?) and also works as a straight compliment to the lawyer's patience. The ambiguity is the joke. 'Nine times. You're a saint.' is one of the tightest three-word payoffs in the episode. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: 'Nine times? — I just had to get rid of him. — Sure.'
jokes.setup: The Priest heard Fleabag say nine times at the door. He asks.
jokes.punchline: 'I just had to get rid of him' — her explanation that nine orgasms were simply an expedient dismissal tool. His 'Sure' — one word, entirely flat, accepting this explanation at face value or choosing not to pursue it.
jokes.explanation: The Priest's 'Sure' is doing enormous work — he either believes her or is choosing the most polite available response to news that she had nine orgasms with a man she's just sent away to spend time with him. Either reading is funny. The calm acceptance of 'sure' is perfect Priest character comedy.

jokes.text: 'I can't be physical with you. — What, we can't even wrestle?'
jokes.setup: The Priest says he can't be physical with her — a serious statement about his vows and their situation.
jokes.punchline: 'What, we can't even wrestle?' — Fleabag's response reframes 'physical' as encompassing innocent sport, knowing full well what he means. It's a deflection by absurdity.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's standout lines. 'We can't even wrestle?' is brilliant for its specificity — of all the ways to deflect 'I can't be physical with you,' choosing 'wrestle' is perfectly calibrated: it's mock-innocent (wrestling is not sex), slightly suggestive (it involves physical contact), and absurd enough to be genuinely funny. It also reveals Fleabag's method: when something is too painful to address directly, she jokes sideways at it. Justifies 8+: original, economical, deeply character-specific.

jokes.text: 'I can't be physical with you.' / 'What, we can't even wrestle?'
jokes.setup: The Priest makes a serious declaration about their impossible situation.
jokes.punchline: 'We can't even wrestle?' — Fleabag deflects from the emotional gravity of the moment with the most absurd possible interpretation of 'physical.'
jokes.explanation: 'We can't even wrestle?' is among the episode's finest jokes. The Priest delivers a serious, emotionally loaded declaration ('I can't be physical with you'); Fleabag responds by taking 'physical' at its most literal and most absurd. The word 'wrestle' is specifically funny — not 'hug' or 'touch' but *wrestle* — because it's the furthest possible point from the erotic while still being technically physical. The joke also functions as a defense mechanism: Fleabag can't process what he's saying so she makes it ridiculous. Character integration is a 9 because only she would do this, and only at this moment. Comparably excellent to the benchmark 8.0 anchor.

jokes.text: 'No, priests have sex, you know. A lot of them actually do. They don't burst into flames, I Googled it.'
jokes.setup: The Priest has said he can't be physical. Fleabag is making her case for why this need not be true.
jokes.punchline: 'They don't burst into flames, I Googled it' — the research is the joke. She's actually Googled 'do priests burst into flames if they have sex,' which is simultaneously practical, absurd, and reveals how much she's been thinking about this.
jokes.explanation: This is among the episode's very finest jokes. 'I Googled it' applied to the question of priestly combustion is perfect on every level: the absurdist premise (bursting into flames), the research commitment (she actually did this), the slightly defensive delivery ('you know, a lot of them actually do'), and the reveal that she's been homework-ing this situation. It's deeply character-specific (Fleabag preparing her argument for sleeping with a priest the way someone might prepare a work presentation), original, economical, and completely quotable. Comparable to the highest-caliber anchor examples. Justifies near-9 on craft.

jokes.text: 'I can't have sex with you because I'll fall in love with you. And if I fall in love with you, I won't burst into flames, but... my life will be fucked.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag has said they won't burst into flames. The Priest reframes why sex is dangerous.
jokes.punchline: 'I won't burst into flames, but... my life will be fucked.' — he accepts the Google research and replaces her joke-premise with an equally devastating but structurally parallel true one.
jokes.explanation: The single best joke in the episode and a contender for the best joke in the series. The callback structure is perfect: Fleabag sets up 'they don't burst into flames' as a joke-argument for why it's safe; the Priest turns it into the most devastating romantic declaration in the show by keeping the image but replacing the consequence. 'I won't burst into flames, but... my life will be fucked' is: (1) a callback (flames), (2) a romantic confession, (3) a tragic statement about his faith, (4) funny in its register (deadpan vulgarity — 'fucked' — after lyrical buildup), (5) structurally elegant (acknowledges the joke and subverts it). This belongs in the same tier as the calibration anchors. Absolutely justifies the top craft score in the episode.

jokes.text: 'We're gonna have sex. — I'm supposed to love one thing. — Oh, my God, we're gonna have sex. — For fuck's sake! Stop that!'
jokes.setup: Fleabag is having an aside to camera ('We're gonna have sex') while the Priest is confessing his dilemma.
jokes.punchline: The Priest breaks the fourth wall — 'For fuck's sake! Stop that!' — he can SEE her looking at the camera. This is the show's first break of the meta-conceit where another character notices Fleabag's direct address.
jokes.explanation: One of the most technically brilliant moments in contemporary television comedy. The entire show is built on Fleabag's fourth-wall breaks as her private language with the audience — a form of intimacy that excludes the other characters. When the Priest says 'For fuck's sake! Stop that!' and clearly sees her doing it, the formal conceit of the series is cracked open. This is not just a funny moment — it's the show's central romantic climax expressed through its formal device being violated. The comedy works because: (1) the audience has been 'in on it' for two seasons; (2) the Priest seeing it implies he truly sees her; (3) 'For fuck's sake! Stop that!' is the most mundanely delivered version of 'I SEE YOUR SOUL.' The impact score is 10 because in a room watching together, this moment would produce gasps, laughter, and possibly tears simultaneously. This is television at the level of 'Snip, snap! Snip, snap!'

jokes.text: 'I don't think you want to be told what to do at all. I think you know exactly what you want to do. If you really wanted to be told what to do, you'd be wearing one of these.'
jokes.setup: The Priest is making an argument about Fleabag's relationship to authority and desire.
jokes.punchline: He gestures to his collar — 'wearing one of these' — a priest's collar as proof of surrendering autonomous desire. Slightly undercut by Fleabag's 'Women aren't actually allowed... — Oh, fuck off! I know!'
jokes.explanation: The collar as argument is a good piece of character comedy — a priest using his vestments as rhetorical evidence. The beat where Fleabag starts 'women aren't actually allowed' (to wear collars) before he preemptively dismisses it with 'Oh fuck off, I know' is a nice reversal of the usual dynamic where she disrupts him.

jokes.text: 'We're gonna have sex, aren't we? — (long beat) — Yeah.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag asks the question plainly. The Priest pauses.
jokes.punchline: His 'Yeah' after a nine-second implied pause — the most understated capitulation to fate possible. The entire weight of the episode's romantic arc lands in one word.
jokes.explanation: The episode's emotional climax delivered in one syllable after a long pause. 'Yeah' is funny in the context of all the theological weight that's preceded it — 'yeah' is not the word of a man surrendering his sacred vows, which is exactly why it works. The anticlimax of a single flat 'yeah' to answer 'we're gonna have sex, aren't we' after twenty minutes of anguished avoidance is impeccably structured. Justifies 8+: maximum economy, perfect structure, and only possible in this character's voice.

tmdb_overview: Claire has a crisis and turns to Fleabag for help, whilst a familiar face makes a reappearance at the cafe. Hilary makes a friend, Martin demands answers, and Godmother and Dad’s wedding hangs in the balance.
